18: veahavta lereiacha kamocha ani YHWH: 'You shall love your neighbor as yourself: I am YHWH.' The neighbor-love command concludes with the divine self-identification formula (ani YHWH) — the obligation is grounded not in a social contract but in YHWH's own character and authority. The scope of rea (neighbor) in Leviticus 19 extends throughout the chapter to include resident aliens (v. 34: 'love him as yourself, for you were aliens in Egypt'). Jesus's parable of the Good Samaritan expands the neighbor-category to include the unexpected outsider, but the Levitical warrant is already broader than its surface reading.
1: YHWH spoke to Moses — the revelation formula opens the Holiness Code's ethical summit, ch 19, which gathers the widest range of social, ritual, and agricultural legislation under a single theological principle.
2: Qedoshim tihyu ki qadosh ani YHWH Eloheichem — be holy (qedoshim) for I, YHWH your God, am holy. This is the foundational axiom of Lev 17–26: holiness is not primarily ritual separation but participation in the divine character. The plural qedoshim addresses the entire congregation (edah) of Israel, not only the priests; holiness is a communal, not merely sacerdotal, vocation.
3: Each of you shall revere (tira'u) his mother and his father, and you shall keep my Sabbaths — the two primary relational loyalties (family and divine rhythm) are placed at the head of the chapter's legislation. The reversal of the Decalogue's order (mother before father, Exod 20:12: father first) is deliberate, noting the mother's authority deserves explicit acknowledgment.
4: Do not turn to idols (elilim) and do not make for yourselves cast gods — elilim is a wordplay on el (God/strength): idols are non-gods, nothings. The plural command echoes the Decalogue's first two commandments (Exod 20:3-4), which the chapter's structure deliberately recapitulates.
5: When you offer a peace offering (zevach shelamim) to YHWH, you shall offer it so that you may be accepted — the peace offering legislation (vv.5-8) enforces the time limit from Lev 7:15-18: the fellowship offering must be eaten within two days; eating on the third day renders it an abomination (pigul).
6: It shall be eaten on the day you offer it or on the day after, and anything left over until the third day shall be burned up with fire — the two-day window (day of offering + next day) applies to the toda (thanksgiving) variant (Lev 7:15) and the neder/nadavah (vow/freewill) variant (Lev 7:16); burning the remainder prevents fermentation and ritual contamination.
7: If it is eaten at all on the third day, it is tainted (pigul); it will not be accepted — pigul (foul, abomination of ritual impurity) is a technical term for an offering rendered void by improper timing or wrong intention; it cannot generate atonement for the offerer even if consumed.
8: And everyone who eats it shall bear his iniquity (avono yissa), because he has profaned what is holy to YHWH, and that person shall be cut off (venikhreta) from his people — eating pigul activates the karet penalty, which applies to high-handed violations of sacred boundaries throughout Leviticus. The eating of a ritually void sacrifice extends its defilement to the eater.
9: When you harvest the harvest of your land, you shall not reap your field right up to its edge (pe'ah sadekha), nor shall you gather the gleanings after your harvest — the pe'ah (corner/edge) law requires leaving unharvested strips at field edges; the leket (gleaning) law prohibits picking up stalks that fall during reaping. Both provisions create a legal agricultural safety net for the poor and sojourner.
10: And you shall not strip your vineyard bare (lo te'olel), neither shall you gather the fallen grapes of your vineyard. You shall leave them for the poor and for the sojourner — the vineyard parallel to the field law (v.9): olel (small clusters missed in harvest) and peret (individual fallen grapes) must be left. Ruth 2 exemplifies the gleaning system working as intended: Boaz's field follows these laws.
11: You shall not steal (lo tignovu). You shall not deal falsely (lo techachedu). You shall not lie (lo teshakru) to one another — three verbs form a progression from action (theft) to relational act (dealing falsely) to speech (lying). The placement after the gleaning laws and before the oath laws connects economic, relational, and verbal integrity as a single ethical domain.
12: You shall not swear by my name falsely (lo tishave'u bishmi lashaqer), and so profane the name of your God — invoking YHWH's name in a false oath treats the divine name as a guarantee for a lie, profaning (cillel) the Name's character. This is the Decalogue's third commandment (Exod 20:7: do not take the name of YHWH in vain) restated in oath-context form.
13: You shall not oppress your neighbor (lo ta'ashoq) or rob him. The wages of a hired worker shall not remain with you all night until the morning — withholding wages overnight exploits the day-laborer who depends on daily payment for daily food (cf. Deut 24:14-15; Jas 5:4). The prohibition on oshek (oppression) and gazal (robbery) frames wage theft as economic violence against neighbors.
14: You shall not curse the deaf (lo teqallel cheresh) or put a stumbling block before the blind (ve-lifney iver lo titten mikhshol), but you shall fear your God — exploiting vulnerability that the victim cannot perceive (the deaf cannot hear the curse; the blind cannot see the obstacle) is precisely what fear of God prohibits. The two examples represent all forms of taking advantage of those who cannot self-defend.
15: You shall do no injustice (lo ta'asu avel) in judgment. You shall not be partial to the poor (lo tissa feney-dal) or defer to the great (lo tehdar feney gadol), but in righteousness shall you judge your neighbor — judicial impartiality is required in both directions: not showing pity to the poor (which distorts justice) and not deferring to the powerful (which sells justice). Tzedek (righteousness) requires consistent standard regardless of social status.
16: You shall not go around as a slanderer (lo telech rachil) among your people, and you shall not stand up against the life (dam) of your neighbor — rachil (slanderer/gossip, literally 'trader/peddler') suggests someone who traffics in others' reputations. The second clause (lo ta'amod al-dam re'ekha, do not stand against your neighbor's blood) prohibits either false accusation that could result in execution or failure to act when a neighbor's life is in danger.
17: You shall not hate your brother in your heart (lo-tisna et-achikha bilvavekha). You shall reason frankly with your neighbor (hokheach tokhiach et-amitekha), lest you incur sin because of him — the internal dimension of community ethics: the heart-hatred that never erupts in overt conflict but festers. The antidote is direct, honest rebuke (tokhach) rather than suppressed resentment — a theme central to both Matt 18:15-17 and rabbinic ethics (Avot 2:10).
19: You shall keep my statutes (chuqqotai tishmoru). You shall not let your cattle breed with a different kind (kil'ayim). You shall not sow your field with two kinds of seed (sha'atnez), nor shall you wear a garment of cloth made of two kinds of material — the kil'ayim (two-kinds, hybrid) prohibitions: animal crossbreeding, mixed-seed planting, and mixed-fiber weaving. These laws enforce created categories (Gen 1: each after its kind), treating the blurring of boundaries as a violation of creation order rather than a mere ritual taboo.
20: If a man lies sexually with a woman who is a slave, assigned to another man but not yet ransomed or given her freedom, a distinction shall be made — the slave-concubine case: she is betrothed or designated but not yet legally free. The case is treated as a crime requiring biqqoret (inquiry/investigation), with a guilt offering but not death, because her slave status means she does not have the full legal capacity to be held to the capital standard of Lev 20:10.
21: He shall bring his guilt offering (asham) to YHWH, to the entrance of the tent of meeting — the asham is the reparation offering for violations involving sacred property or rights (Lev 5:14-6:7). The man has violated the woman's assigned betrothal-right; the guilt offering covers this reparable violation.
22: And the priest shall make atonement for him with the ram of the guilt offering before YHWH for his sin that he has committed, and he shall be forgiven for the sin that he has committed — the standard atonement-forgiveness formula; despite the severity of the violation (sexual exploitation of a protected woman), atonement is available through the asham, unlike the cases in Lev 20 that carry the death penalty.
23: When you come into the land and plant any kind of tree for food, you shall regard its fruit as forbidden (areltem orlato). Three years it shall be forbidden to you; it must not be eaten — orlah (foreskin/uncircumcision) is the term for the fruit of years 1–3; treating it as uncircumcised (forbidden, consecrated away) allows the tree to establish root structure without premature harvesting. The agricultural wisdom is embedded in purity language.
24: And in the fourth year all its fruit shall be holy (qodesh hillulim), an offering of praise to YHWH — the fourth year's fruit is entirely consecrated to YHWH, brought to Jerusalem (cf. Deut 14:23) as first-fruit acknowledgment. Hillulim (praise, celebration) links the fourth-year offering to rejoicing rather than mere obligation.
25: But in the fifth year you may eat of its fruit, to increase its yield for you — the five-year cycle (1-3 forbidden, 4th consecrated, 5th for eating) creates a development sequence: YHWH receives first; then the farmer benefits. The promise lehosif lachem tevuato (to increase its yield for you) grounds the delayed gratification in covenantal blessing.
26: You shall not eat any flesh with the blood in it (al ha-dam). You shall not interpret omens (lo tenacheshu) or tell fortunes (lo te'onenu) — blood-eating prohibition (cf. Lev 17:10-14; Deut 12:16) precedes the divination prohibitions; both treat illegitimate access to life-force (blood) and forbidden knowledge (omens) as violations of divine sovereignty over life.
27: You shall not round off the hair on your temples (lo taqifu pe'at roshechem) or mar the edges of your beard (ve-lo tashchit et pe'at zeqanekha) — mourning rites involving specific beard/hair cutting are prohibited (cf. Lev 21:5; Deut 14:1); these were associated with pagan mortuary practices. The prohibition preserves Israelite distinctiveness from Canaanite grief rites.
28: You shall not make any cuts on your body for the dead (seret la-nefesh) or tattoo yourselves (uktovat qa'aqa lo titenu bachem); I am YHWH — gashing the body for the dead (cf. 1 Kgs 18:28 for the prophets of Baal) and permanent body marking both associated with pagan mourning/devotion practices. The divine self-identification formula (ani YHWH) terminates the clause, grounding the prohibition in covenant identity.
29: Do not profane your daughter by making her a prostitute (lo techallel et-bitkha lehaznotah), lest the land fall into prostitution and the land become full of depravity (zimah) — sacred or cultic prostitution (common in Canaanite fertility religion) would have been the proximate reference; but the prohibition extends to any exploitation of one's daughter for sexual gain. The connection to the land becoming full of zimah links sexual ethics to territorial holiness (cf. Lev 18:24-28).
30: You shall keep my Sabbaths (et-shabtotai tishmoru) and reverence my sanctuary (miqdashi tira'u); I am YHWH — Sabbath observance and sanctuary reverence form a paired refrain (cf. Lev 26:2) that frames the ethical legislation of the Holiness Code; time (Sabbath) and space (sanctuary) are the two axes of holy order around which all ethical life is organized.
31: Do not turn to mediums (ha-ovot) or necromancers (ha-yidde'onim); do not seek them out to be defiled by them — ov (medium/spirit of the dead) and yidde'oni (familiar spirit/wizard) are the two terms used in Lev 20:27 (capital offense) and 1 Sam 28:7-8 (Saul's consultation at En-dor). Consulting the dead usurps YHWH's monopoly on revelation and knowledge.
32: You shall stand up before the gray head (mipenei seva taqum) and honor the face of an old man (ve-hadarta peney zaqen), and you shall fear your God — respect for the elderly is grounded in fear of God, not merely social convention; the same divine self-identification formula (ani YHWH) that grounds the prohibition on idolatry also grounds respect for the aged. The community's attitude toward its most vulnerable members reflects its theological character.
33: When a stranger (ger) sojourns with you in your land, you shall not do him wrong (lo tonu oto) — the ger (resident alien, sojourner) lacks clan protection; the prohibition on oppression (yanh) is the first of a paired command, with the positive counterpart in v.34. Israel's own sojourn in Egypt is the experiential ground for sojourner ethics throughout the Torah.
34: You shall treat the stranger who sojourns with you as the native among you, and you shall love him as yourself (ve-ahavta lo kamocha), for you were strangers in the land of Egypt — the sojourner-love command uses the identical formulation as the neighbor-love command of v.18 (ve-ahavta le-re'akha kamocha). The application of the universal principle to the specific case of the outsider expands the covenant community's ethical radius.
35: You shall do no wrong (lo ta'asu avel) in judgment, in measures of length or weight or quantity — the just-weights legislation (vv.35-36) applies the anti-injustice principle of v.15 (judicial integrity) to commercial transactions; avel (injustice) covers both legal and commercial fraud, treating dishonest scales as a form of perversion of justice.
36: You shall have just balances (mozney tzedeq), just weights (avney tzedeq), a just ephah (eifah tzedek), and a just hin (hin tzedeq); I am YHWH your God who brought you out of the land of Egypt — four instruments of honest commerce (balance, stone weight, dry measure, liquid measure) named explicitly; the exodus formula grounds commercial ethics in the same redemptive identity that grounds every other covenantal obligation. Dishonest trade is not merely illegal but a repudiation of the redeemer God.
37: And you shall observe all my statutes (kol-chuqqotai) and all my rules (kol-mishpatai) and do them; I am YHWH — the Holiness Code chapter closes with a summary command to keep all the statutes and rules, followed by the foundational self-identification. The pairing chuqqim (statutes, non-transparent commands) and mishpatim (judgments, rationally transparent civil laws) encompasses the full range of Levitical legislation.
